front: Figure: this Republican former Civil War general succeeded Andrew Johnson as 18th U.S. president
Figure: nicknamed "Unconditional Surrender," playing on his U.S. initials, after his Civil War demand at Fort Donelson
Figure: won reelection as president in 1872 over Horace Greeley
Figure: the only sitting U.S. president to give a deposition in a criminal trial
Figure: declined to seek third presidential terms in both 1876 and 1880 after the Credit Mobilier and Whiskey Ring scandals
Battle: forced Simon Bolivar Buckner's surrender here in February 1862 by demanding "unconditional surrender," bringing this general to Lincoln's attention
Event: besieged and captured this Mississippi River city in 1863
Battle: won this campaign against Braxton Bragg, including the assault on Missionary Ridge, after which Lincoln promoted him to Lieutenant General, a rank previously reserved for George Washington
Battle: fought Robert E. Lee at the Battle of the Wilderness and this next battle of the Overland Campaign, at a courthouse
back: Battle of Spotsylvania Courthouse (Ulysses S. Grant)

front: Figure: this Seneca sachem served as his military secretary and drafted the Appomattox surrender terms, later becoming the first Native American Commissioner of Indian Affairs
back: Ely S. Parker (served under Ulysses S. Grant)

front: Event: accepted Robert E. Lee's surrender here to end the Civil War; Lee arrived in full dress uniform while this man wore a rough traveling coat
Event: issued General Order No. 11, expelling Jews from the Department of the Tennessee, ostensibly to suppress the black-market cotton trade
Figure: replaced Attorney General Ebenezer Hoar with Amos Akerman and created this new DOJ office (first held by Benjamin Bristow) to prosecute the Ku Klux Klan
back: Solicitor General of the United States (created under Ulysses S. Grant)

front: Figure: suspended habeas corpus in several South Carolina counties to combat Klan violence
Figure: his Secretary of War, William Belknap, took kickbacks from traders at this Oklahoma fort
Figure: Carl Schurz led a breakaway faction of this president's party called the Liberal Republicans, protesting his administration's corruption
Event: the collapse of Jay Cooke's banking house triggered this 1873 financial panic, dooming the latter half of Grant's presidency
Law: Grant signed these 1872 and 1874 revenue acts, also called the Anti-Moiety Acts, cutting the number of U.S. treasury agents after the Sanborn incident
back: Revenue Acts of 1872 and 1874 (Anti-Moiety Acts, under Ulysses S. Grant)

front: Figure: his Secretary of State, Hamilton Fish, negotiated this 1871 treaty settling the Alabama Claims with Britain
back: Treaty of Washington (1871, under Ulysses S. Grant)

front: Figure: sought to annex this Caribbean country (also called the Dominican Republic); the treaty was rejected by the Senate after opposition from Senator Charles Sumner
back: Santo Domingo annexation (rejected under Ulysses S. Grant)

front: Figure: fired ambassador John Lothrop Motley after the Senate rejected his Santo Domingo annexation plan
Event: Grant sent an expedition under John Rodgers to this Korean island, resulting in a failed attempt to establish trade relations and an armed clash
Figure: dispatched the USS Alaska to end the Grebo-Liberian War
Figure: after his presidency, served as an envoy to Japan at Prince Gong's urging to help settle a dispute over these islands, which he later personally arbitrated between China and Japan
back: Ryukyu Islands dispute (Ulysses S. Grant's world tour)

front: Figure: journalist John Russell Young chronicled his post-presidency world tour in a book called Around the World with General ___
Figure: during his world tour, the Maharajah of Jaipur tried to hospitably lose a billiards game to him, but he was so bad he lost anyway
Event: this railroad-finance company paid bribes to Republican politicians (including Grant's running mate Henry Wilson) in a scandal revealed during his presidency
Event: distillers bribed treasury agents to avoid liquor taxes in this scandal, which implicated Grant's personal secretary Orville Babcock and was investigated by Benjamin Bristow
Event: Jay Gould and James Fisk attempted to corner the gold market on this day in 1869, early in Grant's presidency
Figure: his Secretary of the Interior, Columbus Delano, and Secretary of the Navy, George Robeson, were both caught taking bribes for contracts
Figure: his administration was the first to suffer this postal-contract fraud scandal, named for a mail-delivery designation
back: Star Route scandal (under Ulysses S. Grant)

front: Figure: evidence was stolen from a Washington D.A.'s safe, per informant Columbus Alexander, to discredit testimony during his administration
Figure: after leaving office, this financier swindled Grant and his son out of their entire savings in a Ponzi scheme
Work: this autobiography, written while its author was dying of throat cancer, focused mostly on the Civil War rather than his scandal-filled presidency
back: Personal Memoirs of Ulysses S. Grant

front: Figure: this author's publishing house financed and published Grant's memoirs to support his family after the Ferdinand Ward swindle
Figure: this early female attorney petitioned Grant to be admitted to practice before the Supreme Court bar
Figure: his 1880 visit to this New York institution's namesake lakeside site helped spur its growth into a national lecture/education movement
back: Chautauqua Institution (visited by Ulysses S. Grant)

front: Figure: reportedly gave a guest named Horace Norton an exploding cigar as a prank, which Norton kept on display for years
Figure: his scandal-tarred reputation was revived starting in the 1950s by this biographer
Figure: this biographer of Alexander Hamilton published a Grant biography in 2017
